retrieved_docs.title: Occipital Nerve Block -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The GON block can be performed unilaterally or bilaterally. However, no method has been identified as superior to the other. [15] The following steps will discuss how to perform the GON block:

retrieved_docs.title: Local Anesthesia Techniques in Dentistry and Oral Surgery -- Technique or Treatment -- Mental and Incisive Blocks
retrieved_docs.content: Although the mental and incisive nerves are anesthetized by an IANB, mental and incisive blocks are useful when bilateral anesthesia is desired on or anterior to the mandibular premolars. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Celiac Plexus Block -- Technique or Treatment -- Posterior Para-Aortic Approach
retrieved_docs.content: A unilateral block approach, particularly on the left side, has demonstrated reliability for single-sided interventions. However, in cases where a unilateral block proves insufficient, a bilateral approach may be warranted to achieve the desired therapeutic effect.

retrieved_docs.title: Transabdominal Plane Block -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Providing analgesia after an abdominal wall procedure in various abdominal surgeries is an indication of the TAP block. The TAP block can be performed for open abdominal surgeries and laparoscopic procedures. The block is an easier and less risky substitute for epidural anesthesia in postoperative pain control for abdominal surgeries. A unilateral block is used for a one-sided procedure, such as appendectomy, cholecystectomy, nephrectomy, and renal transplant. Bilateral blocks are used for midline and transverse abdominal incisions, such as ventral hernia repair, umbilical hernia repair, exploratory laparotomy, colostomy closure, cesarean delivery, hysterectomy, radical retropubic prostatectomy, bariatric surgery, inguinal hernia repair, and laparoscopic surgery. TAP blocks can also play a role in chronic pain management. [7] [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Transabdominal Plane Block -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The TAP block can be used for postoperative analgesia management in open and laparoscopic abdominal surgeries and inpatient and outpatient surgical procedures. [1] Unilateral left- or right-sided blocks are used for unilateral surgical procedures, such as cholecystectomy, appendectomy, nephrectomy, or renal transplants. In contrast, bilateral TAP blocks are used for midline and transverse abdominal incisions, such as umbilical or ventral hernia repair, cesarean deliveries, hysterectomy, and prostatectomy. TAP blocks are part of multimodal pain management for abdominal surgeries, which adds analgesic benefit to patients, reducing postoperative opioid requirements. TAP blocks are typically placed intraoperatively, either before the surgical incision or at the end of the procedure before emergence from anesthesia. The TAP block's efficacy depends on the spread of LA across the interfacial plane. Newer tissue plane blocks, like the quadratus lumborum block, provide somatic and visceral analgesia.

retrieved_docs.title: Local Anesthesia Techniques in Dentistry and Oral Surgery -- Technique or Treatment -- Inferior Alveolar Nerve Block (IANB)
retrieved_docs.content: The pterygotemporal depression is located between the pterygomandibular raphe and the coronoid notch of the mandibular ramus. The coronoid notch is the most concave point on the anterior ramus, and it can be palpated with the non-dominant hand before the injection. The needle is inserted 20 to 25 mm until the bone of the crista endocoronoidea is contacted. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Pudendal Nerve Block -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The most common cause of pudendal block failure is not giving the anesthetic sufficient time to take effect. If a bilateral block is only effective on 1 side, an additional 5 mL of anesthetic can be administered to the unaffected side. Up to 50% of all pudendal nerve blocks may fail on at least 1 side, although the median failure rate averages about 20%. [30] [31] This is often due to poor technique or a lack of physician training or experience. [31] [32] [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Transabdominal Plane Block -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Traditionally, these landmark blocks have blind endpoints (pops), making their success unpredictable. In 2006, O'Donnell introduced the term TAP block and modified Rafi's original description by advocating for a double pop technique. In 2007, Hebbard et al. proposed using US guidance to identify the intermuscular planes and using the midaxillary line (lateral approach) instead of the triangle of Petit. In 2010, Lee et al. demonstrated that the subcostal approach covered an increased number of dermatomes (4 vs 3 by the lateral approach) and yielded a higher sensory blockade (T8 vs T10). [4] [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Occipital Nerve Block -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Notably, the response to the GON block commonly varies from patient to patient, and the outcome can be challenging to predict. When a GON block is successful, pain typically improves after 20 to 30 minutes and can last for several hours to several months.

retrieved_docs.title: Lower Extremity Blocks -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: FNB has shown superior analgesia in knee surgery compared to intra-articular local anesthetic infiltration after anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) surgery. FNB has also been effective in providing pain relief after tibial osteotomy and meniscectomy. The block can prolong motor blockade when added to spinal anesthesia but does not affect the time to ambulate under general anesthesia. Complications associated with the block include decreased quadriceps strength, inadvertent intraneural injections, and neuropathy.

retrieved_docs.title: Digital Nerve Block -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Four-sided ring block: This method is an extension of the 3-sided block. This technique is less favored because of the potential for ischemic complications. After the 3-sided block is performed, a third injection is performed. Insert the needle at the lateral aspect of the digit on the volar/plantar side and advance it medially as the anesthetic is slowly injected. Wing block : In cases where only the distal part of the digit is affected, this is a suitable alternative to a digital block. Place the extremity with the volar/plantar side facing downward. Hold the needle perpendicular to the long axis of the digit and at a 45-degree angle to the plane of the sterile field. Insert the needle 3 mm proximal to an imaginary point where a linear extension of the lateral and proximal nail folds would intersect. Administer the anesthetic along the proximal nail fold. Withdraw the needle and redirect it toward the lateral nail fold. If needed, repeat this step on the opposite side of the nail.

retrieved_docs.title: Lower Extremity Blocks -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The distal SNB, more commonly referred to as the popliteal sciatic nerve block, administers the injection of local anesthetic about 6 cm above the popliteal fossa before the nerve divides into the tibial and common peroneal branches. This technique is especially advantageous for surgeries involving the lower extremity below the knee. For a successful PSNB, the patient is usually placed prone, with the leg to be treated slightly flexed to expose the popliteal fossa. After identifying crucial bony landmarks, a high-frequency linear ultrasound probe is positioned transversely in the popliteal fossa, revealing the sciatic nerve between the biceps femoris and semimembranosus muscles. With ongoing ultrasound guidance, an echogenic needle is introduced, either from the lateral or medial side, based on the patient's anatomy and the practitioner's preference. It is then directed toward the sciatic nerve. Possible complications with the PSNB include severe pain, hematomas, and temporary neuropathy. [24] [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Deep Peroneal Nerve Block -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Blocking the deep peroneal nerve will provide anesthesia to the interdigital cleft between the first and second toes of the foot. While it does not provide a large amount of sensory innervation, it is still important for patients who are undergoing foot or ankle procedures, such as a bunionectomy. The increased prevalence of ultrasound has also allowed easy visualization of the nerve and increased the success rate of onset to ankle block. While blocking the deep peroneal nerve and the other nerves of the ankle during surgery, it has been shown this nerve block can improve outcomes in the patient's pain postoperatively. This nerve block can also be performed successfully using the extensor hallucis brevis musculotendinous junction as an anatomical landmark. [1] [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Occipital Neuralgia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: It remains a common practice to utilize a landmark-only approach when performing greater and lesser occipital nerve blocks. For blockade of both nerves, medication is infiltrated along the nuchal ridge. While easy to perform and relatively safe if done correctly, this technique may not be particularly accurate and, as a result, could theoretically increase the risk of a false-positive result. To improve accuracy, ultrasound-guided techniques were developed. The original ultrasound-guided technique for injection of the GON (utilized routinely by this article's editor for diagnostic injection and cryoablation) was described by Greher et al. in 2010; it targets the nerve as it courses superficial to the obliquus capitis inferior muscle at the C1-C2 level. [8] There are several advanced interventional procedures in clinical use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2607
retrieved_docs.content: clinical data systems. Twin gestation (gestation twin) is another condition that is clinically associated with the Databases, reflecting the need for specialized data collection in multiple‑pregnancy scenarios. Overall, the clinical landscape connects birth, delivery methods (especially cesarean section and its low transverse variant), membrane integrity, gestational timing (preterm versus term), scar characteristics, contraindicating diseases such as ketorolak, and the systematic documentation of these relationships within Databases. Understanding these interrelationships is essential for optimizing obstetric care, anticipating complications, and guiding evidence‑based clinical decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1021
retrieved_docs.content: organizes general obstetric procedure categories (إجراء توليدي, Antepartum Procedures, Obstetrics incision) and specific clinical actions—ranging from diagnostic sampling and membrane management to fetal stimulation, reduction of multiple gestations, and surgical treatment of ectopic pregnancies—providing a comprehensive framework for coordinating obstetric care across the ante‑partum, intrapartum, and postpartum periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal and fetal conditions that influence labor and delivery encompass a broad spectrum of obstetric complications, uterine functional abnormalities, cervical pathology, multiple‑gestation pregnancies, fetal size disproportion, abnormal fetal presentations, and drug contraindications in pregnancy. Abnormal fetal presentations—breech malpresentation, face or brow presentation, mentum (chin‑first) presentation, transverse or oblique lie, arm presentation (سوء المجيء الجنيني, تدلّي ذراع الجنين), shoulder presentation (وضعية المَجيء الكتفي), funic presentation, and other malpositions or unstable fetal positions—can impede vaginal descent and often require operative delivery or specialized obstetric management; they are linked to prolonged first‑stage or second‑stage labor, failure to induce labor, uterine hypotonia (رحم ناقص التوتر), secondary uterine inertia (عطالة رحمية ثانوية), and prolapse of the fetal arm. Anterior and posterior asynclitism represent specific uterine functional

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 752
retrieved_docs.content: essential for clinicians to anticipate potential delivery complications, decide when normal delivery procedures are feasible, and implement appropriate interventions to optimize maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: The mentum presentation of the fetus, in which the fetal chin is the leading part during delivery, is classified as a مّجيء وَجهي (face presentation). Recognizing this presentation is important for obstetric assessment and delivery planning, as it influences the choice of birth method and the management of potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2038
retrieved_docs.content: Previous cesarean section complicating pregnancy or childbirth is recognized as a specific manifestation of a previous c-section, indicating that a prior cesarean delivery can create complications in subsequent pregnancy or childbirth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1288
retrieved_docs.content: and the English phrase “maternal care for other type of scar from previous cesarean delivery,” indicating that both the presence of a prior cesarean section and the need for maternal care related to any scar from that surgery are understood under the same underlying clinical entity of a previous cesarean operation. Collectively, these relationships illustrate that pelvic‑organ abnormalities, cervical insufficiency, and uterine scarring—whether described in English, Dutch, or Arabic—are interrelated factors that influence maternal care decisions and are associated with an increased risk of labor complications. Recognizing the hierarchical and cross‑lingual connections among these concepts supports comprehensive assessment and management of pregnant patients with a history of cesarean delivery or pelvic‑organ pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
retrieved_docs.content: constitute major complications of pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium. Recognizing the placement of each specific condition within its broader category supports comprehensive assessment, risk stratification, and coordinated management of pregnant patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 145
retrieved_docs.content: to the broader concept of fetal distress that influences maternal treatment (ضائقة جنينية تؤثر في علاج الأم), highlighting the bidirectional impact of fetal and maternal conditions. Maternal pregnancy complications as a whole are nested within a larger, unclassified group of maternal complications (مضاعفات الحمل التي تصيب الأم، غير مصنف في موضع آخر), emphasizing that many of these disorders are interrelated and may require coordinated assessment, risk stratification, and management across the ante‑partum, intrapartum, and post‑partum periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 898
retrieved_docs.content: temporal occurrence during the fetal period, providing a detailed framework for understanding how diverse maternal and fetal conditions can impact labor and delivery outcomes and guiding clinicians in assessing and managing these complex scenarios.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: assessment, early identification of abnormal presentations, cervical pathology, uterine functional disturbances, and multiple‑gestation status, together with avoidance of teratogenic drugs, are essential for individualized management strategies that optimize maternal and neonatal outcomes during labor and delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2607
retrieved_docs.content: highlighting a relationship between this specific surgical technique and early‑term delivery outcomes. Preterm birth (ولادة قبل الأوان) is considered a child outcome of birth and is clinically associated with several factors: it is linked to the unknown cesarean‑section‑scar type (CAESAREAN SECTION SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN), to low transverse cesarean section, and to data repositories (Databases) that capture relevant clinical information. The unknown scar type is also clinically associated with preterm birth, underscoring the importance of scar characterization in managing early deliveries. Post‑delivery residuals (نواتج متبقية من الحمل) occur after birth and are clinically associated with both birth and the Databases, indicating that these postpartum findings are documented and studied within clinical data systems. Twin gestation (gestation twin) is another condition that is clinically associated with the Databases, reflecting the need for specialized data collection in multiple‑pregnancy

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2360
retrieved_docs.content: Medical databases are clinically associated with several obstetric conditions, including تمزق الأغشية الباكر (premature rupture of membranes), نواتج متبقية من الحمل (residual pregnancy outcomes), and gestation twin (twin gestation). These associations indicate that such databases contain relevant clinical data on the incidence, risk factors, and management of these conditions. Additionally, the low transverse cesarean section technique is clinically associated with تمزق الأغشية الباكر, suggesting a link between this common surgical approach and the occurrence, diagnosis, or treatment of premature rupture of membranes. Recognizing these relationships helps clinicians leverage database information to improve decision‑making and patient care in obstetric practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2071
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy-Related Procedures comprise a range of interventions used to manage complex obstetric conditions and facilitate safe delivery. Among these, the surgical treatment of ectopic pregnancy and abdominal pregnancy represents critical operative approaches for addressing abnormal implantation of the embryo, thereby preventing life‑threatening complications for the mother. In addition, Vaginal Assisted Delivery is included as a procedural option that supports the progression of labor and assists in the birth of the infant when additional support is required. Together, these child procedures under the umbrella of Pregnancy-Related Procedures illustrate the spectrum of surgical and assisted techniques employed in modern obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1894
retrieved_docs.content: The category “MATERNAL AND FETAL CONDITIONS AFFECTING LABOR AND DELIVERY” includes a range of specific child conditions that can markedly influence the course of childbirth. Among these are breech malpresentation that has been successfully converted to a cephalic presentation, septuplet pregnancy, حمل بأربعة توائم (a pregnancy with quadruplets), حمل توأمي (twin pregnancy), and سوء تموضع الجنين (fetal malposition). These entities represent complex fetal presentations and multiple‑gestation pregnancies, each requiring specialized obstetric management to optimize maternal and neonatal outcomes during labor and delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 179
retrieved_docs.content: حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ (bicornuate/abnormal uterine pregnancy), قلة السائل الأمنيوسي (oligohydramnios), مخاض قبل الأوان (preterm labor), نواتج متبقية من الحمل (residual pregnancy outcomes), gestation twin, and بضع الرحم (uterine rupture), which is linked to early placental separation. مضاعفات الحمل (Pregnancy Complications) is a parent concept for اضطراب جنيني (Fetal Disorder) and is classified as “التأثير المتأخر لمضاعفات الحمل والولادة والنفاس” (Late Effects of Pregnancy, Delivery, and Postpartum Complications). Specific manifestations within مضاعفات الحمل include تمزك الأغشية الباكر, مخاض قبل الأوان, and تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر (a broader related condition). Cardiac and central nervous system complications of anesthesia or sedation during labor and delivery are narrower relationships to مضاعفات الحمل and are coded as “Other and unspecified complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother,” emphasizing their impact on postpartum maternal care. Procedural and scar factors are
